title: Solar power diverters explained: heating water with surplus solar
description: How solar power diverters send surplus PV to your immersion heater, what each diverted kWh is really worth, and when exporting at SEG rates pays more.
blurb: What a solar diverter does, what each diverted kWh is really worth, and when exporting your surplus pays more.
lede: A solar power diverter is a small box that watches the electricity leaving your house and, the moment your panels generate more than you are using, sends the surplus into your hot water cylinder's immersion heater instead of out to the grid. The pitch is simple: free hot water from sunshine you were giving away. Whether it stacks up in 2026 depends almost entirely on two numbers, and neither of them is on the diverter's box: what you currently pay to heat your water, and what your export tariff would have paid you for those same units.
short_answer: A solar diverter costs roughly £400 to £800 installed and typically sends 1,000 to 1,500 kWh of surplus solar a year into your hot water cylinder. Each diverted kWh saves you the cost of heating that water another way, about 8.6p with a gas boiler or 26.11p with a peak-rate immersion heater, but costs you the export payment you give up, typically 5p to 15p. It is a strong buy if you heat water electrically at daytime rates, a reasonable one if you get little or nothing for exports, and a money-loser if you have a gas boiler and a 15p export tariff.

sections.h2: What a solar power diverter is and how it works
sections.html:
A diverter is a power-control unit, usually fitted near your consumer unit, with a small clamp (a CT sensor) around the meter tail. The clamp watches the flow of electricity in real time. Whenever your panels generate more than the house is using, the spare power would normally flow out to the grid. The diverter intercepts it and feeds it to the immersion element in your hot water cylinder instead.

The clever part is proportional control. A standard immersion heater is a 3 kW all-or-nothing load, and switching it on with a simple timer would pull in expensive grid electricity every time the sun dipped behind a cloud. A diverter modulates the power continuously: if your surplus is 400 W, it sends 400 W to the element, and if the surplus drops it backs off within a second or two. You import nothing extra; the element simply runs at whatever the sunshine spares. The cylinder's own thermostat still cuts everything off once the water is up to temperature.

In effect, your cylinder becomes a heat battery. Instead of selling surplus units for a few pence and then buying gas or peak-rate electricity later to heat water, you store the energy as hot water and use it that evening. If you are still getting to grips with how generation, self-use and export fit together, start with our guide to how solar panels work.

sections.h2: What you need before a diverter makes sense
sections.html:
The shopping list is short, but every item on it matters:

  • A hot water cylinder with an immersion element. This is the deal-breaker. A combi boiler heats water on demand and has no cylinder, so there is nowhere to put the diverted energy. Roughly half of UK homes are combi-only, and for them a diverter is a non-starter.
  • Solar PV with a real surplus. A 3.5 to 4 kW array generating 3,400 to 4,000 kWh a year will export a healthy chunk of that on sunny days. A small 1 to 2 kW array in a home with high daytime use may have too little spare to bother capturing.
  • An accessible meter tail. The CT clamp needs to sit on the live cable near your meter, and the diverter needs a wired or wireless link back from it. This is a job for an electrician, not a long one.

One common mix-up: a diverter is not solar water heating. Solar thermal uses dedicated roof collectors full of fluid to heat the cylinder directly, and is a separate (and pricier) installation. A diverter piggybacks on ordinary PV panels you may already own.

It also pays to fix the cylinder itself first. Hot water you capture for free still leaks away through a bare or thinly wrapped tank, so check our guide to cylinder jackets and pipe lagging before spending on electronics.

sections.h2: What a diverted kWh is actually worth
sections.html:
This is where most diverter marketing goes quiet. Every kWh you divert does two things at once: it saves you the cost of heating that water some other way, and it costs you the export payment you would have received for sending it to the grid. The value of a diverter is the gap between those two numbers, and the gap varies enormously between households.

With a modern gas boiler at around 85 per cent efficiency, a kWh of hot water costs about 8.6p in gas (7.33p divided by 0.85). With a peak-rate immersion heater it costs the full electricity unit rate of 26.11p. Set those against typical Smart Export Guarantee rates of 5p to 15p per kWh and you get this:

How you heat water nowAvoided cost per kWhNet value at 5p exportNet value at 15p export
Gas boiler (about 85% efficient)8.6p+3.6p-6.4p (you lose money)
Immersion heater at the standard day rate26.11p+21.1p+11.1p
Immersion on a cheap overnight EV tariff (7p example)7p+2p-8p (you lose money)

At 26.11p per kWh electricity and 7.33p per kWh gas, Ofgem cap July to September 2026, with SEG export examples of 5p and 15p per kWh. Checked June 2026.

Now scale that by a realistic year. A family with a 4 kW array and normal hot water demand might divert 1,200 kWh annually. On a peak-rate immersion with a 5p export tariff, that is worth about £253 a year. The same 1,200 kWh in a gas-heated home on a 15p export tariff is worth minus £77: you would have been better off selling the electricity and burning gas. And if you are not signed up to any export tariff at all, every diverted kWh is pure gain, worth about £103 a year against gas and £313 against a daytime immersion.

The method for your own house is simple: take your water-heating cost per kWh, subtract your export rate per kWh, and multiply by 1,000 to 1,500. If the first number is smaller than the second, stop here and keep exporting.

sections.h2: Diverter or battery: where should the surplus go?
sections.html:
A diverter and a home battery compete for the same surplus units, but they pay you back very differently. A battery stores spare solar and releases it as electricity later, so each stored kWh displaces a 26.11p grid import. A diverter turns the same kWh into hot water, which in a gas-heated home displaces only 8.6p of gas. Per unit, the battery wins comfortably.

Per pound spent, it is much closer. A diverter costs £400 to £800 fitted; a usable home battery system typically costs several thousand pounds. The diverter can pay for itself in two or three years in the right house, while solar battery storage usually needs the better part of a decade. They also solve different problems: a battery covers your whole evening electricity demand, while a diverter only ever addresses hot water.

The two can coexist. Most modern setups let you set a priority order, and the usual logic runs: power the house first, fill the battery second, heat the cylinder third, export whatever is left. Flip the diverter ahead of the battery only if your water heating is electric and expensive. If you are weighing all of this up before buying panels at all, our guide on whether solar is worth it covers how export earnings, self-use and add-ons interact.

sections.h2: Costs, installation and how much surplus you will actually capture
sections.html:
The diverter unit itself typically costs £300 to £550, and an electrician will usually fit one in an hour or two for £100 to £250. There is no scaffolding, no roof work and no change to your panels or inverter, which is why it is the cheapest solar add-on there is.

How much it captures is bounded by two things: your surplus and your cylinder. A typical household uses 3 to 5 kWh a day heating water, and once the cylinder hits its thermostat setting the diverter has nothing left to do, so excess beyond that still exports. From roughly April to September a 4 kW array will heat most or all of a family's water; through the winter the panels barely cover the house load and the boiler or immersion does the work as before. Over a full year, 1,000 to 1,500 kWh diverted is a sensible planning figure for a 4 kW system, less for smaller arrays or homes with heavy daytime use.

Two practical points worth knowing. First, every diverter has a boost function, so you can still force a full tank from the grid or your cheap overnight rate before guests arrive. Second, hygiene: a cylinder that sits warm but not hot can harbour legionella bacteria, so the water should reach 60°C regularly. Decent diverters include a scheduled weekly boost for exactly this; make sure it is switched on. For what a forced heat-up costs, see our immersion heater running cost guide.

A solar diverter is one of the most polarised purchases in home energy: brilliant value in one house, quietly loss-making next door. The decision tree is short.

  • You heat water with an immersion or electric boiler at daytime rates: buy one. At roughly 21p net per diverted kWh you are looking at £250 or so a year and a payback inside three years.
  • You are on the legacy Feed-in Tariff: buy one. FiT export payments are deemed at 50 per cent of generation rather than metered, so you get paid the same whether you export or divert. Diverted energy is pure gain.
  • You have a gas boiler and no export tariff: reasonable. About £100 a year means a four-to-eight-year payback, similar to a lot of insulation measures.
  • You have a gas boiler and a 10p-plus export tariff: skip it. Exporting pays more than the gas you would avoid burning, and the maths gets worse every time export rates rise.
  • You have a combi boiler: it is not an option, with or without enthusiasm.

One last check before committing: SEG export rates move around, and a tariff switch can flip the sums above in either direction. Check the current Smart Export Guarantee rules and rates on Ofgem's official SEG page, and your own supplier's export offer, before you order anything.

faq.q: Can I use a solar power diverter with a combi boiler?
faq.a: No. A diverter needs a hot water cylinder with an immersion element to store the energy in. A combi boiler heats water on demand and has no cylinder, so there is nowhere for the surplus to go. Your alternatives are a home battery or simply a better export tariff.

faq.q: Does a solar diverter work alongside a home battery?
faq.a: Yes. Most systems let you set a priority order, and the usual arrangement is house load first, battery second, hot water third, export last. A stored kWh offsets 26.11p of grid electricity while a diverted one usually offsets about 8.6p of gas heat, so the battery normally deserves first claim unless your water heating is electric.

faq.q: Is there a legionella risk if the water only gets warm on cloudy days?
faq.a: There can be if the cylinder sits lukewarm for long periods, since legionella bacteria multiply between about 20°C and 45°C. The fix is to make sure the water reaches 60°C regularly. Most diverters include a scheduled weekly boost from the grid for this purpose; check it is enabled.

faq.q: I am on the old Feed-in Tariff. Will diverting reduce my export payments?
faq.a: No. Most domestic FiT installations are paid deemed export, assumed at 50 per cent of everything you generate, regardless of how much you actually send to the grid. You keep the full payment whether the energy is exported or diverted, which makes a diverter unusually attractive for FiT households.

faq.q: How much of my hot water will a diverter actually provide?
faq.a: With a 3.5 to 4 kW array, expect most or all of your hot water from roughly April to September, tapering to very little in midwinter. Over a full year that typically works out at 1,000 to 1,500 kWh diverted, against a typical household water-heating demand of 3 to 5 kWh a day.
